example: Rehearsing and memorising: repeatedly working though lists of words or fact, memorising key terms to help with recalling important content
Practice Testing: Self-testing or taking practice tests over to-be-learned material; Quizzing with flashcards
Recalling material such as key terms, ideas, and concepts, either freely or based on clues; recalling anything remembered about the material
Spacing study over time; Implementing a schedule of practice that spreads out study activities over time
Reviewing material studied in earlier sessions later
Distributing items like questions or practice problems within a study session

Mixing together different types of items, questions, or problems; Implementing a schedule of practice that mixes different kinds of problems, or a schedule of study that mixes different kinds of material, within a single study session
Rereading: Restudying text material again after an initial reading
Marking potentially important portions of to-be-learned materials while reading by highlighting or underlining
Writing summaries (of various lengths) of to-be-learned texts; writing down the main points from a text; creating outlines of the to-be-learner material; creating flashcards
Keyword mnemonies: Using keywords and mental imagery to associate verbal materials when studying vocabulary or facts
Restating main ideas of a lesson in own words
Creating mind maps/knowledge maps, content maps or graphic organisers; converting spoken text into spatial arrangements (diagrams, tables)
Creating explanative or organisational drawings to illustrate lesson contents
Imagining/forming mental images of lesson content; While studying, creating a mental image of the learning material
Producing explanations by answering 'why' questions; asking and answering questions about the material
Teaching and preparing to teach/explain the to-be-learned material to someone else; peer tutoring
Self-explanation to make sense of the material, explaining how new information is related to known information
Thinking aloud: explaining steps taken during problem solving
Discussing to-be-learned material in a group
Goal setting: Breaking down a learning goal inte steps required to reach it, prioritising, creating to-do lists
Self-evaluation or self-questioning: checking in on one's own understanding during learning

Regulation: monitoring, controlling, and making predictions; checking for mistakes and ensuring the process is successful; reflecting on one's own learning processes and adjusting them if necessary. Adapting learning strategy use to the desired goal and to personal preferences.

Environmental structuring and preparation: picking a suitable environment, e.g. desk at home or at the library; adjusting the learning environment to be as optimal as possible, eliminating distractions, clutter, noise etc., ensuring that required material is within easy reach
Self-consequences or rewards. Taking regular breaks; Decreasing stress by using relaxation or exercise.
Seeking and selecting information: Seeking out further literature and sources of information such as books, online resources
Seeking social assistance: asking for help from peers, tutors, or others such as family and friends. Making use of online forums.
Managing demand and attention: switching between memorising and trying to understand depending on the context, e.g. to construct understanding later based on facts that have been memorised.
Planning and time management. Making plans or to-do lists, taking other commitments into account. Using Pomodoro-Technique, interspersing working periods with breaks. Reducing interruptions.
Self-commitment: Committing to regular study times alongside relaxation breaks
